zh-hans.lifeSummary: William de BovileжШѓSahala de BovileеТМE MRS SAHALA DE BOVILEзЪДеДње≠РпЉМдЇО1175еєіеЗЇзФЯеЬ®иЛ±еЫљиґКеПѓзИЊгАВдїЦиЗ≥е∞СжЬЙ1дЄ™еДње≠РгАВдїЦеОїдЄЦдЇО1264еєіеЬ®иЛ±еЫљдЇЂеєі89е≤БгАВ

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names ¬© Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dƒУ вАШthighвАЩ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Bovile
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names ¬© Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin): habitational name from any of several places in Normandy whose medieval forms share a similar range of variants, such as Bouville (Seine-Maritime), any of several places called Bi√©ville (Calvados, Manche) or Biville (Manche, Seine-Maritime), Beuzeville-la-Giffard (Seine-Maritime), Boiville (Orne), and perhaps Boudeville (Seine-Maritime). See also   Boswell   and   Beville  .

variants.name: Boswell
meanings: English and Scottish (of Norman origin): habitational name from Beuzeville-la-Giffard in Seine-Maritime, France, named with the ancient Germanic personal name Boso + ville вАШsettlementвАЩ. The final element has been altered as a result of association with the common placename ending -well вАШspring, streamвАЩ. Some instances of Bovill may have the same origin.

variants.name: Beville
meanings: English (Middlesex and Surrey; of Norman origin): variant of   Bovill  . Derivation from Beuville (Calvados) is also possible, though unproven.

French (also B√©ville): habitational name from B√©ville (Moselle) or B√©ville-le-Comte (Eure-et-Loir), or a variant of Beuville, a habitational name from Beuville (Calvados). The surname B√©ville is rare in France.

variants.name: Buffalo
meanings: English: apparently an American English nickname from buffalo, perhaps denoting a large, powerfully built man. Alternatively, perhaps an altered form of an unidentified surname, possibly English Bovill . Compare Buffaloe and Buffalow .

Native American: translation into English (and shortening) of a personal name based on a word, such as Lakota and Dakota Sioux tata≈Лka and Cheyenne hotoa'e, meaning вАШbuffaloвАЩ or вАШbullвАЩ. See also Bull 5.

History: Majority of the Buffalos trace their origin to the brothers Arthur and William Buffelow, who were recorded in NC in 1750s. Their surname was (later) also spelled Buffalow, Buffaloe, and Buffalo. The word buffalo is even nowadays often misspelled buffelow.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names ¬© Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil вАШwill, desireвАЩ + helm вАШhelmet, protectionвАЩ. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the вАШconqueredвАЩ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Magna Carta
historicEvents.description: King John of England agreed to a charter of rights called the Magna Carta. The document was originally drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to establish peace between King John and a group of rebel barons. The Magna Carta was completed in 1215 and is now considered to be one of the greatest constitutional documents of all time.

historicEvents.title: France Declares War on England
historicEvents.description: France declared war on England as Louis VIII of France attacked Poitou and northern Gascony.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

en.lifeSummary: William de Bovile was born in 1175, in Yorkshire, England as the son of Sahala de Bovile and E MRS SAHALA DE BOVILE. He had at least 1 son. He died in 1264, in England, at the age of 89.
